summary:
The Boxer Rebellion (1899–1901) was an anti-foreign, anti-Christian uprising in China led by members of the Yihetuan ('Righteous and Harmonious Fists'), a secret society that Western observers called the Boxers. The movement targeted Christian missionaries, Chinese Christian converts, and foreign nationals, and briefly threatened to expel the Western powers from China before an international military expedition crushed it and imposed the punishing [[event:3443|Boxer Protocol]].

The Boxers emerged in the context of deepening Chinese humiliation and social distress. The defeats of the [[event:2540|Opium Wars]], the catastrophe of the [[event:2543|Taiping Rebellion]], the defeat by Japan in 1895, and the subsequent [[event:2586|scramble for concessions]] had created widespread resentment of foreign presence and influence. Drought and economic hardship in northern China in the late 1890s fueled recruitment into the Boxer movement, which taught that its members could be made invulnerable to bullets through spiritual practice.

The Boxer uprising spread rapidly through the northeastern provinces in 1899–1900. Boxers and then regular Qing troops besieged the foreign legation quarter in Beijing for 55 days in the summer of 1900, while thousands of Chinese Christians were massacred across the region. The Empress Dowager Cixi, whose court had initially tried to suppress the Boxers, declared war on all foreign powers in June 1900 — a decision widely regarded as catastrophically miscalculated.

An international expedition of approximately 20,000 troops drawn from eight nations — Britain, Germany, Russia, France, the United States, Japan, Austria-Hungary, and Italy — lifted the siege of the legations in August 1900 and occupied Beijing. The subsequent looting of the capital and reprisal killings were extensive. The [[event:3443|Boxer Protocol]] imposed in September 1901 required China to pay an enormous indemnity of 450 million taels of silver (to be paid over 39 years), execute senior officials, allow foreign troops to be stationed between Beijing and the coast, and demolish certain fortifications — terms that further weakened the already fragile Qing state and contributed to the revolution that ended the dynasty in 1912.
